Thank. All right, welcome. Thank you First Levi, doc, Braley, officials, uh, and everybody else. Very good to have you here. Have you here for six hours, although we won't be doing it on one big block. There will be some breaks. So, um, look, I'll, I'll open it up to to you for some introductory remarks.

And then, um, we will then move on with our structured agenda, which was firstly around organizational performance, been around key service areas, performance and challenges, and then health infrastructure. Thank you, professor Levy. Dr. Braley. Thank You. Thank you. Um, chair your committee. Uh, we'll just make some introductory comments.

I'll just start off briefly and then Gram will take over from me and we'll just make some introductions. I've got, um, Dr. Andrew Connolly, our vice chair with me, Dale Brand Chief Executive, and Bevin Mackenzie, our Chief financial officer. And Dale and Bevin have got a number of our colleagues, management colleagues with us, and they've got the areas of, uh, that they lead and they've got special expertise and we'll involve them in, uh, dealing with the questions as well.

Uh, so just, uh, for a sense of context, uh, health New Zealand is a significant and extensive organization. It's the largest organization in New Zealand that has a total expenditure of 29 billion and a workforce of over 100,000. We deliver integrated services right across the country.

This is treatment, uh, diagnostic care and, um, Preventive. Uh, we work across 87 campuses, 1,273 buildings and 11,092 beds. We also fund primary and community sector to the value of 10.1 billion. That's across 9,000 providers and 15,700 contracts.

Um, and our services, uh, uh, cover the whole spectrum of integrated care. Um, in the global context. Uh, our, um, health service still performs, uh, quite well comparatively. We've got a life expectancy that's currently at 82 years. Uh, we rank 16 in the world for life expectancy.

Um, we still have disparity in life expectancy of Maori, although, uh, pleasing in recent years. The gap has reduced from eight to six years since 2002. Uh, obviously in all of this luck in most of the areas, more work is required. Uh, thank you Stan. Uh, just a little bit about our context.

Uh, health New Zealand has been going through a prolonged period of change for the last three years. We're now starting to enter a more stable period, uh, including with our senior appointments now, uh, in place. Uh, I just wanted to acknowledge it has been a difficult, uh, industrial climate over the last year, and we're also operating in an environment of significant increase in demand for services.

And, for example, our emergency departments last year saw 4% uplift. Uh, clinical quality and safety, obviously a very significant priority for Health New Zealand. We have done longitudinal reviews looking at the last 10 years around quality and safety, where we see a mixed picture, a number of indicators improving, some stable, but some we clearly have some more work to do.

Just in terms of performance. We have seen some significant movement in the last year on the financial, uh, front. We have managed to come in within budget with a small surplus to our budget position for the first time since our inception. We've also seen movement on our health targets with all five health targets improving by the end of Q4.

Uh, so that is pleasing. The elective boost was achieved. Uh, we've also, after reaching historic highs in January, February, started to see the reduction in our overall waiting list, which is very pleasing, meaning that New Zealanders, uh, having to wait less length of time for access to services, uh, on productivity and overall, uh, how our services are performing.

We've also seen a change in productivity in the last year. The first change that we've seen in a number of years, uh, where we look at our case rated discharges. Uh, so that, again, also appeasing significant change in primary and community care going on, uh, with enrollments, uh, into P hos now over 5,050,000 people added, uh, to those enrollments, GP and primary care services, the very foundation of our healthcare system, more than 21 million visits, uh, in the last financial year.

At the same time, we acknowledge that access to some services has been difficult, and so trying to improve access, improve the amount of urgent care available as well. Uh, just another thing to mention in the primary and community care space, which is pleasing, is obviously if we can move to prevention and a focus on screening, which identifies disease at a very early stage, uh, we get better health outcomes.

And we have seen some very significant improvements in our screening programs. Cervical, uh, cancer screening, for example, hit 74%, which is a very big uplift. Our bowel cancer screening is sitting around 57 to 58%. And also our breast, uh, cancer screening programs, uh, doing well, uh, close to the 70% target on the Maori health front.

And we have sala here, and I'm sure we'll get questions around Maori Health. As Leicester has said, we have seen a reduction in the life expectancy gap. Uh, we do monitor health target performance specifically for Maori, and another, a number of other metrics. And we have seen, uh, for example, that we still have a lot of work to do, uh, with things like immunization for Maori, where the Maori rate is 68% compared to the total population rate of 82%.

Finally, on workforce, we have seen an increase in our healthcare workforce, uh, with a lift over the last two years of around 6% in our medical staffing, 12% in our nursing and allied health, and 7% in mid midwifery and support services. Our vacancy rates and our turnover rates are now at historic lows.

Uh, and as we know, uh, there's always more demand for healthcare services. So we are continuing to look at ways to invest in workforce, and we recently announced, uh, the intake of nursing graduates 1800 over the, uh, coming months into Health New Zealand.

Um, and with that, um, hand over to you, Mr. Chair. Thank you. Thank you very much. Uh, we have a question here from Dr. Campbell. Excellent. Thank, thank you very much. Um, we saw with the, the merger of the district health boards with an extra layer bureaucracy on top that the financial systems weren't, weren't well thought through.

And we have heard that the finances were operated through a single Excel spreadsheet. Can you give us an update, uh, on the financial management improvements that Health New Zealand has undertaken? Point of order, Point of order. Dr. Verl, Uh, I believe my colleague is misquoting the Deloitte Report, um, into the financial systems at Health New Zealand.

And perhaps you'd like to more truthfully characterize I Was, well, it's been point of order. I wasn't actually referring to the Deloitte report, so I did not mention it, um, in my question. Well, then where is your evidence for that statement? It, it was in the media. I'll let the question stand and if it's untrue, then Health New Zealand can say.

So We have seen a very significant change in our financial performance as an organization, as you'll see in the annual report result, where we had come in, uh, on budget this year with a very small surplus the preceding two years, we did not. We did have a Deloitte review, which members will be well aware of, uh, and we have worked very hard to implement the findings of the Deloitte review, including such things as moving to, uh, system across all of our districts.

We also move to more closely match revenue to expenditure, to work with our districts to build our budgets so that people are very aware of, uh, the budgets that they have allocated to them and around areas of significant costs. Uh, we have, uh, had much greater oversight of those costs as the system has continued to develop.

I would just say in the context, this was, uh, the biggest merger in New Zealand, uh, history, uh, with a number of legacy systems, including financial systems that were brought together. Uh, and we are still working to continue to improve some of this, but we have our CFO here.

I'm not sure if you'd like to add anything else, Ben. It's a very good summary, Dale. Um, as you say, the FM system went live in June 24. That was a number of years work, so we now have single integrated financial system, which consolidates all of our, uh, results. Um, I would notice, well that the way that we manage performance has improved.

So we have integrated operating performance reviews with all of our regions and our major delivery functions every month. Those then cascade through, uh, the organization. Uh, we have daily cash flow forecasting, which was also one of the issues that we saw, uh, pre previously. Uh, we've improved and got greater clarity around the delegations and, uh, we're continuing to improve that, uh, as well.

Um, and the final point I would mention is that the, um, putting in place the teams and having stability of the people in the organization, we've made a number of, uh, permanent appointments both to Dale's executive team. Uh, we have regional chief financial officers in all of the, uh, regions that's been in place since, uh, July.

We do have more work to do on that front, but having consistent capable people working through a single source of truth systems is obviously very important to good financial report T Supple metric. Um, what progress has been made with activity based costings and budgets? Uh, we have improved with our 25 26, uh, budget.

So we had a nationally coordinated, uh, activity, uh, planning. Uh, I would note that, um, organizations of the scale of Health New Zealand, that normally takes a number of years to get to, uh, detailed local activity planning. Um, so I would say that improvement was made through the 25 26, uh, budget.

We will continue to improve that, in particular in the coming year through, um, even greater, uh, involvement from frontline in defining those activity plans. We recognize that that is not yet as detailed as it should be. Uh, we expect that that would probably be over the next two or three years that we would see that get to the point, um, that it should be Supplementary Supplementary. Dr. Barrow, Can you please, um, elaborate on when the work to implement FPM began?

That was a project over a number of years. I dunno when that was started, but it was certainly a multi-year, uh, project significant, uh, investment. Well, perhaps I could help you. It began under the last government. That's correct, isn't it? Yes. So the f him, uh, system that you cite as a solution to the issues raised in the Deloitte's report was actually being implemented prior to the Deloitte report being written?

That's correct. The, the, the system, uh, I can't confirm the date, but I'll take you with you for that. Yeah. Uh, went live in, in June, 2024, and the Deloitte reports obviously are later that year. Um, I would note as well that we continue to improve that system. It's not a one and done. So ongoing improvements being made To of, and you mentioned the benefits of having consistent people enroll, um, for the financial management of Health New Zealand.
Can you remind me who fired all the people from the roles previously of the Senior Executive of Health New Zealand?

No one was fired, uh, in the leadership team. Many people, um, uh, ceased as a result of the development of devolution, uh, where, for example, there would be one person running hospital systems across the whole country. They were paid, uh, what they were due in the employment contracts. So If we return to my colleague's question here, the two benefits that you cited in terms of the current management of Health New Zealand's, uh, financial system, with the consistency of people in role, which is a response to problems created by restructuring done under the present government and the, uh, and a software system that was under development prior

to the current government coming in. I, I think that's, uh, if you have a look at the detail around the, that actually started with, um, uh, before the previous government was the government, before that it was part of the Health Benefits Limited program. I think that, uh, people have changed quite a lot since Health New Zealand started.

There's been a lot of change since 20 22, 23, and that's all been contextual and we've moved past that. And primary. Thank you. I'm trying to understand the myth of the ringfence because it's very difficult to find out how we can measure outcomes when the accounting systems, well, first of all, the organization's Health Quality Safety Commission, the ministry, the minister, everyone points at Health New Zealand.

Health New Zealand doesn't have a line item that talks about $2.7 billion of mental health. In fact, it is incorporated into other things. Why do you not, uh, disaggregate or get to the level of being able to show what the mental health spend is in the regions for specialist services for primary and community health and for Copa Maori?

Because without that, it is very difficult to understand whether we are getting value for money. And yet we've had a 32% increase in the so-called ring fence, which is around $674 million. It also leaves, uh, the system open to double accounting Point. We have a point of order Dr. Campbell.

Um, we have a structured agenda, um, and mental health is actually, um, in, in the next part service area. The very fact that I would be expected to ask financial questions in something around a disaggregated mental health segment shows why it is a poor cousin of the health system.

This is a systems question that I think deserves an answer. When we're looking at the system, We are talking about the, the first area organizational performance. So if we can tie it into to that. Yeah, And I, I think the commissioner understood the question and I'm happy to wait for the answer. Uh, we have five output classes that we report into the annual report that have work and work programs against them.

Correct. Uh, we then, uh, we then obviously report against the key measures of, um, mental health. So you obviously see the mental health, uh, output class, quarterly reporting being, uh, delivered, uh, there.

So it's a, it's a detailed bottom up budget and reporting against it. And then the initiatives, the non-financial, obviously as well Supplementary, if I may, um, the minister referred to the contracting arrangements in Health New Zealand as being the outcome measures. I've looked at many of those contracts, and they are output measures, as you've quite rightly said, the output classes.

Where in the system do we find the outcome measures in one place for mental health, given that there is no specific ringfenced budget in the system, and yet we keep referring to this mythical ringfence? So I think in the annual report, you will see our reporting against mental health and addiction services.

We also, in our quarterly monitoring reports report a number of, uh, metrics related to mental health. Um, a number of those are outputs, and they were also inclusive of access. Um, some measures, uh, quite hard to define in terms of pure outcome and mental health.

And that's why we have both access measures as well as patient reported measures, which are also very important. If you look overall at sort of national, global measures of outcomes, we, you do see an overall improving right back to ex life expectancy, mortality rates. You do see, uh, improvements there, but that you are picking up on something that is difficult to define in terms of specific outcomes.

And so often if you can't get an exact outcome, you have access measures. And those access measures, uh, mean that people can access treatment services, which we know have proven benefit, which then leads to those outcomes. Final question, if I may, Mr. Chair? Yes, you may. Would it, would it be easier to measure the outcomes if there was a line item or a section on mental health rather than having it in the three current class systems?

And secondly, do you accept that there is a risk of double accounting? For example, things that have been funded by MPI and the rural sector have been announced as mental health announcements within the ringfence, when in fact the budget has come from a completely different boat. Uh, do you think it would be easier to organize yourselves so that there is sufficient focus on mental health and sufficient change to be able to be measured that follows the money directly?

I think the current, uh, movement from the various appropriations reflect where service delivery occurs. So either in primary care, public health, or our hospital services, uh, or her Maori. So I mean, those are allocated decisions into buckets.

I mean, you could absolutely potentially re uh, look at that as a potential option. Uh, what we try to do is have measures that relate to each of those areas and those output classes. Uh, but there's, there's an ability to recut that, if that makes more sense.

At the moment. Uh, the way that we deliver the majority of services is thinking about primary community public health, thinking about hospitals, uh, and thinking about, in this case, for Maori as well as a specific appropriation. So I can see the logic of that system as well. Thank you.

So we just ask Phil, Grady to just add a bit more color to these answers, Phil, heads up health. Right. Look, um, and just to, to add to Dr. Brandy's comments is we report quarterly to the Ministry of Health on our RINGFENCE spend. And to Bevan's point, that gets down to a, a granularity of the budget and RC code.

So there is line of sight all the way through. Um, and to your second point, which was around, um, line of sight around where we've got joint funding. So that reporting into the Ministry of Health on a quarterly basis has direct line of sight for the health contribution to that, uh, particular initiative.

And is it fair to say they would be coalesce around the output delivery? Yeah, so output and again, not with rather than outcome. Yeah. Notwithstanding, you know, Dr. Brown's comments around, it's a bit tricky sometimes to get to the bottom of the, um, um, outcomes. But, um, yes, that's where it sits. Just if I could, Mr.

Chair, uh, the point about outcomes is a good point, and we do have an aspiration to shift from output. We'll still have outputs, but we have an aspiration to shift from outputs to outcomes. It is quite challenging, especially wanna get to the clinical section. Our chief medical officer could touch on, uh, the outcomes, but that, that is the destination.
Appreciate that. Thank you. Okay. We have a primary from Dr. Vera. Thank you. Uh, can you please describe the current state with respect to budget and other decision making in hospitals?

So we have, um, we have budgets, as Dale referred to earlier, that have been built up, uh, based on activity plans and which were built up based on not just the regions, but also the other delivery, uh, functions. Uh, those budgets have been devolved to districts and where possible to services within that.

Uh, as I mentioned before, in response to the member's question, we haven't got that yet to the, uh, detailed level of granularity that we would like. Uh, but we do have all of our regions, all of our districts and key service functions with the known, uh, budgets. Uh, and, uh, we track those, as I also mentioned before, on a monthly basis.

Um, thank you. I I, I'm gonna ask a set of questions just to clarify exact exactly what that means. So does that mean that information system has that level of visibility, or are you telling me about who holds the right to plan and spend the budget and make decisions for the services as well?

Uh, both of those things are true. The, uh, obviously the, um, the financial system includes all of the budget information, uh, at the level that I have described, um, in terms of decision rights, that obviously then flows into the delegations that we have in place. Uh, and those are all, uh, clear in the organization as well.

So it's not just in the financial system, but also the delegations matrix that you have, which dictates that. Thank you. Um, I'll just continue. Yes, Mr. Chair. So the, um, information system, does that mean that a head of, um, a group in a hospital, uh, will be able to see the same screen that you can see in terms of their, um, uh, the budget they have and their current spending rate and so on?

Um, they would probably work with their finance utterly to access the screen as, as, as such. But yes, they would be working off the same information, which is, uh, we use the adaptive budgeting tool and that then integrates into the f PIM financial system for, uh, for. And can you describe the delegation matrix for who's making decisions about how money gets spent in hospitals?

Uh, so the, it obviously cascades from the board, uh, through, uh, Dale. Uh, those delegations then flow, uh, into the executive regional directors for, uh, hospitals. Um, specifically ERDs have a $5 million CapEx. Can you spell out for the sake of the record what the ERD is, please?

Oh, executive regional director. So we have the four executive, uh, regional, uh, directors. Uh, for example, Dr. Andrew Paulette Brant is the executive regional director for the northern region, uh, here with us today. Uh, so Dr. Brant, for example, he has a $5 million, uh, delegation on, uh, CapEx. So his team will have formed a capital budget for, uh, the year.

And if those approved projects are within $5 million, then Dr. Brant can proceed with those, uh, projects with no additional approvals, uh, required. So let's, um, uh, take the example of a single hospital. How many people have delegations to spend money in that hospital? Was it just someone as, as senior as Dr. Brant?

5 million probably doesn't reflect most of the, the, uh, very high proportion of the, um, of the budget for a hospital or a district I know. So, uh, we have a delegations policy, which starts with the board's delegation to me, and then cascading down. That all goes all the way down to RC managers.

Um, so those are the, uh, the very local managers that sit. What Does RC stand for, please? Um, responsible responsibility center managers. So those people who have a budget assigned to them, and then they have, uh, rights related to that responsibility center. So that sits in the delegations policy.

What we are currently doing with the movement to, uh, strengthen devolution and to strengthen local services and service delivery is reviewing that delegation policy and strengthening the amount of decision making that will be done locally as we move into this devolution.

I understand that. So to understand, to contextualize whether that is a big change or a small change, I'd just like to understand what, what sort of person is the RC manager? Did you say? Huh? Do they run a single department? Yes. Are they the head of all surgery? You Know, so they would be service managers. They run a service such as, uh, plastics or orthopedics.

They would be charge nurses who run their wards. So they would have the RC for their ward. So they're usually people who have both, uh, people responsibility. Mm-hmm. And those report to them, then they have a delegation around those people, or where they have expenditure need to do clinical equipment supplies that supply their areas, then they have delegations related to that as well.

Can you explain to me, um, people at, at that level in the health system, their situation, um, say in, uh, 2018, in, in the DHB days, would they have a similar level of delegation then? Yes. By and large, they should have very similar levels of delegations, uh, as we go through this next.

And the delegation movements that we are looking at now, uh, uh, sort of at the higher levels of what hospitals will be able to agree on of themselves is the very thing we are looking to raise. But at that very local level, a lot of those delegations relate to their teams and people, which should not have changed very much since 2018.

All the things that they need to order and bring in to support that service, they should be similar. Um, but we'd have to do a direct comparison to, to say if something had changed over time. And, um, we discussed with the minister yesterday that there are some, uh, funds that, um, are retained by people higher up the, uh, management chain in, in Health New Zealand.

What proportion of, uh, funds sit, uh, outside the level of a district? I think in terms of that context yesterday, correct me if I'm not, uh, right here was around the contingency that was held nationally. Uh, yeah, but I'm, I'm using that as an example, right?

I just wanna know the balance of the, uh, people in, in the districts deciding 80% of health New Zealand's spending, or, um, a lot less. Oh, nearly all decision making that occurs in Health New Zealand or where the budget is spent, is spent locally. Uh, the delegations relate to at what level do you need an additional level of scrutiny over that decision to make sure it's value for money?

And that goes back to that delegation policy. So the higher that level is, the more we want to know about the relative return on the investment or the outputs or clinical, uh, outcomes that that will achieve. And so this expenditure is still occurring locally. Understood. I mean, that's truism, isn't it?

We have to spend the money in a place that, but the, um, uh, who gets to decide where to do the decision rights? Where's the balance of decision rights as a proportion of the overall funding? So in terms of the expenditure, the vast majority is absolutely local. It's those really big decisions that you have a new business case for, uh, or a really significant amount of spend, then those decision rights, uh, go higher up the chain.

Uh, now as we go through this devolution, uh, change in policy, we will be lifting those devolution, uh, those, um, uh, delegations much further so that local hospitals will have even higher delegations than they currently have. If I may, just one final point of clarification, Mr. Chair, to finish this question? Yes. Um, the current state is, is that recorded in a document and what is the name of that document?

Yes. So we have a current delegated, um, authority, um, document. And what we're doing now is the modifications to that for the devolution, envi, uh, environment. And that's, that's available. Thank you. Thank you. Um, just so everyone knows, we, we are up to, we have primaries waiting from Amish, myself, Johanna, and Ingrid.

Can I join the list again, please, Mr. Chair? Oh, of course you can. Dr. Vel and Jenny Markoff, that's in this first part, right? Just, that's correct. Brilliant. Thank you. Um, and, and you've talked a lot about the evolution already, um, this morning.

Uh, what progress has been made to move to a regional structure and what still needs to happen? Yes. So, um, if we just go back, the, the most significant change to, um, health New Zealand, of course, was when we, uh, changed in 2022 to 2023 with the, um, uh, the end of the DHB era to the Health New Zealand.

That was the most significant change that we have been through. And then of course, there was this period of consolidation, uh, around the merger and the functions of the merger, uh, when the commissioner was appointed, uh, at that point, the devolution processes started to begin, uh, and that included the establishment of four regions, uh, and the appointment of regional leads into those areas.

Uh, as we have moved forward in recent, uh, months, we've also seen things such as permanent appointments go into those four regions. You've got one of the leads here. Uh, you have also seen how budgets relate to those regions, uh, and those districts and the establishment of regional clinical governance functions, all of which have occurred.

In terms of our structure, we've also seen a devolution of a number of functions. So initially the planning and funding outcomes was a single national function that now is a national function, as well as four regional functions. So in terms of the $10 billion spend that occurs around 8 billion has already devolved into the regions.

We've also been going through processes to evolve our things such as emergency management systems, health and safety systems, back to regions at local districts. Uh, as we move forward, uh, with this next wave, we'll be looking to very much strengthen hospitals, uh, and local districts and DDoS further, but within a view of nationally, uh, national policy, national design, regional co coordination, and local delivery.

So we've been on this journey for some time, and now we look to accelerate, uh, considerably. Uh, and we have had also, uh, our letter of expectations last week, which included, uh, info requests, uh, to Health New Zealand about, uh, accelerating that pace. And so we are actioning that immediately to speed up that evolution even further, I would say, of our enabling services.

And those are things like people, culture, uh, digital services, uh, we aim at 85% of all staff are basically devolved or working locally within districts. If just for, can I just add, sorry, just, um, in, in terms of the devolution, so the devolution will be completed at the end of June of this year.

If you compare that, as Dale said, I initiated that process in, uh, August of last year. So it's about be about a two year process, Ireland as a comparison. I know all systems are different, took five years. NHS is not completed 10 years. Uh, Canada started in 2013, not yet completed.

So ours, our devolution has actually happened quite quickly. The last elements are with Dale as chief executive to finish now before the end of June. There are no further decisions to be taken at a governance level. Uh, there are some risks associated with an accelerated devolution like that, and of course we putting mitigations in place to deal with that.

But, uh, it is, you know, a very quick process as regards the delegations. For example, we expect Dale's delegations as chief executive to be doubled, and that'll filter right through the organization. So people's freedom to make decisions at a local level, which is our aspiration, will be set in place.

The one thing, I guess, and Dr. Brown's question, maybe just to add to that, uh, people have got quite a lot of freedom currently to spend, but nobody, and I don't expect any of you would expect we would do this. Nobody's got the freedom to spend beyond their budget. Okay. So, and that's why this next budget is really important, because this will be probably the first time in history, really, that there's a truly authentic bottom up production plan and budget, because the production plan is as important as the budget.

Excellent, thank you. And just for clarification, did you say that the, um, the devolution process started in August last year? August Last year, Yeah. Excellent. Thank you. What, what, why did, why ha have you decided to take a regional structure? Sorry, I understand. Why, why did we need to go back to a, a regional structure?

Well, the organization's just way too big. Uh, there's too much distance between where the decisions were being made and where the action is happening. Uh, uh, uh, perhaps I should add, when we first spoke about devolution, we weren't talking about simply, um, devolution around management and accounting issues.
